does: This paper introduces compositional adversarial attacks that jailbreak vision language models by pairing adversarial images with generic text prompts. The core innovation is an embedding-space-based methodology that crafts visually benign adversarial images targeting toxic embeddings in the vision encoder, requiring only access to the vision encoder rather than the full language model. The authors develop four distinct embedding space targeting strategies and demonstrate high jailbreak success rates across different VLMs, showing that these cross-modality attacks bypass alignment mechanisms that resist text-only jailbreaks by forcing the language model to draw toxic context from adversarial visual input.
connects: This work extends the landscape of adversarial attack research on aligned language models, building on prior jailbreak studies that exposed vulnerabilities in LLM safety mechanisms. It responds to the observation that multimodal systems introduce new attack surfaces beyond text-only inputs, leveraging insights from vision adversarial examples and embedding space manipulation techniques. The compositional strategy parallels work on combining weak adversarial signals across modalities, while the vision encoder focus connects to studies of CLIP and other foundation models as attack targets. The paper addresses the practical concern that most prior jailbreak work assumed white-box access to full models, extending attacks to realistic black-box scenarios where only vision encoders are accessible.
enables: This research opens directions for studying cross-modal vulnerabilities in increasingly deployed multimodal systems and motivates stronger defense mechanisms that consider vision-language interaction points. It suggests future work on detecting adversarial images in vision encoders, developing alignment techniques robust to coordinated cross-modal attacks, and understanding fundamental limits of aligning multimodal systems. The embedding-space targeting methodology could extend to other vision-language architectures and potentially other cross-modal systems. The work also frames important questions about defending closed-source systems where attackers lack model access, pointing toward the need for external monitoring, input sanitization, and robustness evaluation protocols specifically designed for multimodal alignment scenarios.
